response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), in force since May 2018, applies to any organization handling EU residents’ data and grants rights such as access, erasure, portability, and explicit-consent control, while mandating Data Protection Officers and 72-hour breach reporting. Non-compliance can trigger penalties of up to 4 % of worldwide turnover or €20 million, evidenced by headline fines of €1.2 billion for Meta, €746 million for Amazon, and more than €200 million for Google. GDPR’s model has been echoed in California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act, positioning it as the global benchmark for privacy law. Critics, however, say its costs, cookie-banner proliferation, and uneven enforcement burden small firms and may hamper innovation, even as the regulation cements data privacy as a fundamental right.
